title: How Can I Afford College Textbooks Without Going Broke?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You go to the campus bookstore. They want $300 for a textbook. For one class. For one semester. This is not unusual. It's also not necessary. You can get your required books for a fraction of that price. Here is how.

Never Buy New (Unless You Have To)

New textbooks are a scam. They are overpriced. They become outdated immediately. The only difference between a new textbook and a used one is the price tag. Buy used.

Check the edition. If the professor requires the 5th edition, the 4th edition might be almost identical. Ask the professor. They might tell you the older edition is fine. The page numbers might be different, but the content is usually the same.

International editions are often cheaper. They are legal to buy and use. The paper quality might be lower. The content is the same. Save your money.

Rent, Don't Buy

Chegg, Amazon, and other services rent textbooks. You pay a fraction of the purchase price. You use the book for the semester. You return it. Done.

Renting is ideal for books you'll never look at again. That's most textbooks. Do you really need to own that chemistry textbook forever? No.

Rent early. Prices go up as the semester starts. Order a few weeks before classes begin for the best deals.

The Library Has Copies

The campus library usually has copies of required textbooks. They might be on reserve. That means you can't check them out overnight. But you can use them in the library.

Scan the chapters you need. Take photos with your phone. Or just do your reading in the library. Free.

Pro tip: Some libraries have the previous edition available for checkout. If the professor says the older edition is fine, check it out for the whole semester. Free.

Check for Free PDFs

Some textbooks are available legally for free. OpenStax has free, peer-reviewed textbooks for many introductory courses. Many professors use them.

If your textbook is in the public domain (older than 1928), it's free on Project Gutenberg. That's rare for science textbooks but common for literature.

Library Genesis (LibGen) is a shadow library. It has PDFs of many textbooks. It is legally questionable. Use at your own risk. Many students use it. Be aware that it's not authorized by publishers.

Access Codes Are the Trap

Many courses now require an access code for online homework. You cannot avoid this. You cannot buy a used access code. You must buy new. This is where the money goes.

Check if you can buy the access code separately from the textbook. Sometimes you can. The access code alone is often cheaper than the access code bundled with a physical textbook you won't use.

Check if the access code includes an e-book. Many do. You might not need a physical textbook at all. Buy just the code. Save money.

Ask Upperclassmen

Post in your class Facebook group or Discord. 'Anyone have the textbook for CHEM 101? Willing to sell or lend?' Upperclassmen often have old textbooks they want to get rid of.

Some will give them to you for free. Others will sell for $20. That's better than $200 at the bookstore.

Ask your professor if they have extra copies. Sometimes professors receive free desk copies. They might give you one.

Don't Buy Until You Know You Need It

Many professors list textbooks that are 'required' but never used. Wait until the first week of class. See if the professor actually assigns readings. See if you can borrow a friend's copy.

Some courses require the book for homework problems. Others just list it for reference. You don't need to own a reference book. Use the library copy.

Buying textbooks is a necessary evil. But it doesn't have to bankrupt you. Shop around. Rent. Borrow. Use the library. Keep your money in your pocket.
